candidates.name: Kevin Stitt
candidates.notes: One of the most overtly Christian governors in America. Publicly declared he was 'claiming Oklahoma for Jesus' at a church event (2022). Signed constitutional carry (2019). Issued executive order making Oklahoma 'strongest defender of religious liberty in the nation' (2025) — ordering state agencies to purge barriers between church and state. Backed Bible-in-schools curriculum through State Superintendent Ryan Walters and Heritage Foundation partnership. Filed amicus brief supporting St. Isidore Catholic virtual charter school at SCOTUS. Launched Office of Faith-Based Initiatives. NRA A+ rating. Signed near-total abortion ban. Cherokee Nation citizen. Term-limited in 2026. [2026 actions: Signed 150+ bills across multiple sessions, with ~20 vetoes. Opted into federal tax-credit scholarships (school choice). Continued anti-CRT + parental-rights legislative push.]

profile.background: Businessman and mortgage company CEO (Gateway Mortgage Group); enrolled member of the Cherokee Nation; became 28th governor in 2019.

candidates.name: James Lankford
candidates.notes: Ordained Southern Baptist minister and former director of Falls Creek Baptist Conference Center (largest Christian camp in US, 50,000+ campers annually). Led bipartisan border deal negotiations in 2024 — deal killed by Trump opposition; censured by Oklahoma GOP. Heritage Action dropped to 50% in 118th Congress from 98% in 117th due to border deal vote. Voted for Big Beautiful Bill in 2025. Strong pro-life record (FRC 100%). NRA A+ rating. Open to stronger background checks but opposes assault weapons bans. Authored federal religious liberty protections. Lost significant MAGA support over border deal but remains solidly conservative on life, marriage, religious liberty.

profile.education: University of Texas at Austin (BS Secondary Education), Southwestern Baptist Theological Seminary (MDev)

candidates.office: Governor of Oklahoma (2026 R Candidate · former Speaker of OK House)
candidates.notes: Former Speaker of the Oklahoma House of Representatives (2017-2024). R candidate for OK Governor 2026.

profile.confidence_note: 2026-05-20 — 2026 OK Governor race. Archetype: establishment_r.

claims.text: As Oklahoma House Speaker, authored House Joint Resolution 1034 to expand Second Amendment protections and require courts to interpret the Amendment based on its original language; declared 'The Second Amendment is the pillar upon which all the other amendments rest.'

As House Speaker, steered two landmark anti-transgender measures to the governor's desk: a 2022 statute requiring public school students to use bathrooms matching their birth-certificate sex, and a 2023 law banning gender-transition surgeries and hormone treatments for minors. His 2026 governor ads tout: 'I led the charge to keep boys out of our daughters' locker rooms.'

Responded to the 2023 Oklahoma Supreme Court ruling recognizing a limited abortion right by declaring 'House Republicans will continue to protect the lives of the unborn and pursue legislation that values all life,' pledging continued pro-life legislation.

candidates.name: Greg Treat
candidates.office: Former President pro tempore of the Oklahoma Senate
candidates.notes: OK Senate pro tem 2018-2024 (term-limited). OK SD-47.

profile.confidence_note: 2026-05-20 — State legislative leadership record. OK Senate pro tem 2018-2024 (term-limited). OK SD-47.

claims.text: Co-authored SB 918 (signed April 27, 2021), Oklahoma's abortion trigger law that re-activates the 1910 statute criminalizing procurement of an abortion except for medical necessity upon reversal of Roe v. Wade — protecting unborn life from conception at the earliest legal opportunity.

SB 918 pursues abolition rather than mere restriction: instead of regulating providers or imposing gestational limits, it re-criminalizes abortion entirely under a pre-Roe statute — treating the unborn as rights-bearing persons and offering no regulatory safe harbors for the abortion industry.

As Senate President Pro Tempore, advanced SJR-23 through the Oklahoma legislature, placing a citizens-only voting constitutional amendment on the November 2024 ballot; Oklahoma became the sixth state to put such a measure before voters.
